draft.introduction: Chapter 48 contrasts the pursuit of learning with the pursuit of the Tao. Learning adds something every day; following the Tao removes something every day, until one arrives at non-action.

translation.text: In the pursuit of learning, every day something is added. In the pursuit of the Tao, every day something is dropped. Less and less is done until non-action is achieved. When nothing is done, nothing is left undone. The world is won by those who let it go. When you try and try, the world is beyond winning.

translator_notes.note: This chapter sets up a direct opposition between wei xue (pursuing learning) and wei dao (pursuing the Way). The implication is not anti-intellectual but rather that conceptual accumulation must eventually give way to a process of subtraction. The parallel with apophatic theology—knowing God by progressively negating what God is not—is striking.
